era_context: The Western Roman Empire under Honorius and regent Stilicho is engaged in military campaigns against Alaric's Visigoths and other barbarian groups. The Eastern Roman Empire under Arcadius faces Persian pressure and internal court factionalism. In China, the Eastern Jin Dynasty is in its final decades of decline. Christianity continues to consolidate institutional power across the Mediterranean world. This is a period of acute military and political instability in the Mediterranean and Europe.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asia and the Indian subcontinent (limited contemporary written sources)

events.title: Stilicho defeats Alaric at the Battle of the Frigidus River (or nearby engagement)
events.description: Stilicho, the Roman general and regent, achieves a significant military victory against Alaric's Visigothic forces, likely in the region of the Frigidus River (modern-day Slovenia/Croatia border). This victory temporarily halts Visigothic expansion and demonstrates Roman military capability, though it does not resolve the underlying political crisis. The battle is part of a series of engagements between 395–410 CE in which Stilicho attempts to contain barbarian threats while managing internal Roman political divisions.
events.certainty_note: The exact date and location of this engagement are debated. Claudian's poems provide near-contemporary evidence but are propagandistic. The battle may have occurred in 399 or 400 CE.
cross_references: 394-001 (Battle of the Frigidus River, 394 CE, which may be confused with this engagement)

events.title: Gainas' revolt and massacre of Gothic foederati in Constantinople
events.description: Gainas, a Gothic military commander in the Eastern Roman army, leads a revolt against the Eastern Roman government. The revolt is suppressed, and a massacre of Gothic soldiers and settlers in Constantinople follows. This event demonstrates the acute tensions between barbarian military elites integrated into the Roman army and the Roman civilian and ecclesiastical establishment. The massacre represents a violent rejection of Gothic military dominance in the Eastern Empire, contrasting with the Western Empire's more gradual accommodation of barbarian kingdoms.
events.certainty_note: The event is well-attested in multiple sources, though they are not contemporary. The exact date is sometimes given as 400 CE; 399 CE is also plausible.
cross_references: 400-001 (Alaric's proclamation as king in the West, contemporary with Eastern instability)

events.title: Faxian, Chinese Buddhist monk, begins pilgrimage to India
events.description: Faxian (法顯), a Chinese Buddhist monk, departs on a pilgrimage to India to obtain Buddhist scriptures and study at major monasteries. His journey, which will last until c. 412 CE, is one of the most significant early Buddhist pilgrimages and provides invaluable documentation of Central Asian and Indian geography, politics, and religious life in the early 5th century. Faxian's account (the Foguoji or 'Record of Buddhist Kingdoms') is a primary source for understanding the Silk Road and Buddhist networks.
key_figures: Faxian (Buddhist monk and pilgrim, c. 337–422 CE)

sources.name: Faxian's own account, Foguoji (Record of Buddhist Kingdoms), compiled after his return

events.certainty_note: The date of Faxian's departure is traditionally given as 399 CE, though some sources suggest 400 CE. The pilgrimage itself is well-documented in his own account.

events.title: Eastern Jin Dynasty: Emperor Longxu's reign continues amid factional strife
events.description: The Eastern Jin Dynasty under Emperor Longxu (r. 397–402 CE) faces increasing internal factional conflict among military strongmen and aristocratic families. The imperial court's authority is eroding as regional warlords consolidate power. This period of instability will culminate in the dynasty's collapse and the founding of the Liu Song Dynasty in 420 CE. The fragmentation reflects broader post-Han patterns of regional autonomy and the rise of military aristocracies.
key_figures: Emperor Longxu of Eastern Jin (r. 397–402 CE)
Liu Yu (future founder of Liu Song, b. 363 CE)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: The broad political trajectory is well-documented in Chinese historiography. Specific events in 399 CE are less precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Gainas' revolt is disputed; some sources place it in 400 CE rather than 399 CE. The significance of Gainas' revolt has been reinterpreted by modern scholarship: rather than a simple 'barbarian uprising,' it reflects complex factional politics within the Eastern Roman elite, with Gothic military commanders as one faction among many competing for power. The traditional narrative of 'barbarian invasion' obscures the integration of Gothic elites into Roman administrative and military structures. Faxian's pilgrimage date of 399 CE is conventional but not universally accepted; some sources suggest 400 CE.
historiographic_note: The year 399 CE is poorly documented in surviving sources. Most information comes from later compilations (Zosimus, Socrates Scholasticus) written 50–150 years after the events. Claudian's poems provide near-contemporary evidence for the Western Empire but are propagandistic panegyrics. Chinese sources are abundant but compiled centuries later. The Eastern Roman Empire's perspective is filtered through ecclesiastical sources (John Chrysostom, Socrates Scholasticus), which emphasise religious and moral dimensions over political and military detail. Faxian's pilgrimage is well-documented in his own account, but this is a retrospective compilation. The year 399 CE is a conventional marker in modern historiography; the actual significance of events occurring in this specific year is difficult to assess.

graph_edges.note: Stilicho's victory in 399 CE was part of his broader campaign against Alaric continuing into 400 CE
Gainas' revolt in the East occurred during the same period as Alaric's consolidation in the West
Faxian's pilgrimage beginning in 399 CE culminated in his return to China in 412 CE
The factional strife in Eastern Jin in 399 CE led directly to the dynasty's collapse and Liu Song's founding in 420 CE
